narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on their academic records, test scores, and personal circumstances to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 25 (well under the 36.70 average), Year 1 school decile of 5 (slightly below the 5.71 average), and Year 3 decile of 3 (lower than the 5.53 average), along with your plan to attend full-time like most students—and predicted a 71% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected; law schools use this tool to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar, avoiding risks to their own pass rates and your future career. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were, in order of influence, your LSAT score, your Year 3 school decile ranking, and your Year 1 decile ranking. Your LSAT score had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction, as it was significantly lower than what the model sees in students who typically pass. Your Year 3 decile ranking, placing you in the lower third of your school's performance, also pushed the prediction toward failure by signaling weaker later academic momentum compared to peers. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile in the middle of your class helped counter this somewhat, nudging the odds slightly toward passing since it showed a solid start. Overall, these elements made your profile riskier than the typical successful applicant, whose stronger test scores and consistent high rankings align with bar passers. While full-time status matched the norm and didn't sway things much, it couldn't offset the key drags from your scores and later grades. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average or higher, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your Year 1 potential and resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these improvements—your path isn't closed, just needs refinement.
